chunk_summary: This section discusses diagnostic clues and tissue confirmation for tuberculous and fungal bone infections in India, emphasizing imaging, histopathology, and coordination with national tuberculosis programs.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical presentation and diagnostic challenges of skeletal tuberculosis in India.
Learn the role of imaging and tissue sampling in confirming tuberculous and fungal bone infections.
Recognize the importance of coordinating treatment with national tuberculosis programs.
key_facts: Skeletal tuberculosis often presents with symptoms evolving over weeks to months, modestly elevated inflammatory markers, and imaging findings like vertebral destruction or cold abscess.
The spine is the most common site, but peripheral joints and long bones can also be affected.
Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is critical for identifying epidural extension, cord compromise, and vertebral destruction in spinal disease.
Tissue confirmation through histopathology, mycobacterial culture, and molecular testing is essential for diagnosis and to exclude mimics like tumors or fungal infections.
Coordination with the national tuberculosis program ensures adherence, drug toxicity monitoring, and contact evaluation.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Skeletal tuberculosis should be suspected when bone or joint symptoms evolve over weeks to months, constitutional symptoms are present, inflammatory markers are only modestly elevated, or imaging shows vertebral body destruction, paravertebral collection, cold abscess, or chronic monoarthritis.
document_key_topics: Role of imaging and tissue confirmation
